questions.question: There are 20 identical competitive firms. Each has MC(q)=4+q and minimum AVC below all relevant prices. At market price P=14, what is industry supply?

choices.A: Q=10 because each firm supplies 10 and there is one representative firm.
choices.B: Q=200 because each firm supplies q=10 and quantities are horizontally summed.
choices.C: Q=280 because industry supply equals price times number of firms.

questions.explanation: Each firm sets 14=4+q, so q=10. With 20 firms, Q=200. B is correct. A forgets horizontal summation, C multiplies price by firms, and D treats the number of firms as quantity.

questions.question: An industry has 10 firms with individual supply q1=P-6 and 15 firms with individual supply q2=0.5P-3, valid for P>=6. At P=18, what is total industry supply?

choices.A: Q=120 because only the first group supplies.
choices.B: Q=90 because only the second group supplies.
choices.D: Q=30 because the two individual supplies are added once.

questions.explanation: At P=18, first-type firms supply 12 each, so 120 total. Second-type firms supply 6 each, so 90 total. Industry supply is 210. C is correct. A and B omit one group, and D adds representative quantities without multiplying by number of firms.

questions.question: An industry has 50 identical firms, each with supply q=P-10. At P=20, 10 new identical firms enter. How does industry supply change at that price?

choices.A: It rises from 500 to 600 because the market supply curve shifts right as the number of firms increases.
choices.B: It falls from 600 to 500 because entry lowers each firm's supply.
choices.C: It remains 500 because price is unchanged.
choices.D: It rises from 50 to 60 because only firm count matters.

questions.explanation: At P=20, each firm supplies 10. With 50 firms Q=500; with 60 firms Q=600. A is correct because entry horizontally adds more individual supply curves. B reverses the effect, C ignores the number of firms, and D confuses firms with units.

questions.question: An industry has 40 firms, each with supply q=P-8. Higher input prices shift each firm's supply to q=P-12. At P=24, what happens to industry supply?

choices.A: It rises from 480 to 640 because higher costs increase price needed.
choices.B: It falls from 640 to 480 because each firm's supply falls from 16 to 12.
choices.C: It stays 640 because the market price is unchanged.
choices.D: It falls from 960 to 480 because output is cut in half.

questions.explanation: Initially each firm supplies 16, so Q=640. After the cost increase each supplies 12, so Q=480. B is correct because higher input costs shift individual and industry supply left/up. A reverses the effect, C ignores costs, and D uses a false halving rule.

questions.question: How is the short-run industry supply curve constructed from individual firm supply curves?

choices.A: By vertically adding firms' marginal costs at each quantity.
choices.B: By horizontally adding the quantities each firm supplies at each price.
choices.C: By averaging all firms' average total cost curves.
choices.D: By choosing the lowest-cost firm's supply curve only.

questions.explanation: Industry supply is the horizontal sum of individual supplies: at a given price, add quantities. B is correct. A describes vertical summation, C uses average cost rather than supply, and D ignores other firms.

questions.question: Two identical firms each have upward-sloping supply curves beginning at P=$5. Compared with one firm's supply curve, the two-firm industry supply curve has what graphical relationship?

choices.A: It is twice as far to the right at every price above $5.
choices.B: It is twice as steep at every quantity.
choices.C: It has the same quantities but twice the price.
choices.D: It begins at twice the shutdown price, P=$10.

questions.explanation: Horizontal summation means that at any price above shutdown, total quantity is twice one firm's quantity. A is correct. B and C describe vertical transformations, and D wrongly adds prices rather than quantities.

questions.question: What determines the slope of a short-run industry supply curve in a competitive industry?

choices.A: Only the market demand curve's slope.
choices.B: The slopes of individual firms' marginal cost curves and how many firms are active at each price.
choices.C: Only fixed costs, since firms cannot change plant size.
choices.D: The average of all firms' accounting profits.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Industry supply reflects the horizontal sum of active firms' operating MC curves. A confuses supply with demand, C overstates fixed costs, and D is unrelated to the supply curve slope.

questions.question: A city has 80 food trucks, each with supply q=P-5 for P>=5. A new licensing rule reduces the number of active trucks to 60 but does not change individual costs. Which model predicts the supply effect at P=15?

choices.A: Industry supply shifts left: Q falls from 800 to 600 because fewer individual supply curves are horizontally summed.
choices.B: Industry supply shifts right because fewer trucks mean less congestion.
choices.C: No change occurs because each remaining truck has the same supply curve.
choices.D: Supply becomes vertical because licenses are fixed.

questions.explanation: At P=15, each truck supplies 10. With 80 trucks Q=800; with 60 trucks Q=600. A is correct. B invents a cost effect not given, C ignores the number of firms, and D confuses fixed number of firms with perfectly inelastic output.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes individual firm supply from industry supply?

choices.A: Individual supply gives one firm's quantity at each price; industry supply adds all firms' quantities at each price.
choices.B: Individual supply is horizontal, while industry supply is always vertical.
choices.C: Individual supply is based on demand, while industry supply is based on utility.
choices.D: Individual supply equals total revenue; industry supply equals total cost.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Industry supply is the horizontal sum of individual firm supply curves. B overgeneralizes shapes, C mixes producer and consumer theory, and D misdefines supply.

questions.question: Market supply is Qs=4P-40 for P>=10. At price P=25, what is producer surplus?
questions.explanation: At P=25, Q=60. The supply intercept is P=10, so PS=0.5×60×(25-10)=450. A is correct. B calculates revenue, C uses a rectangle instead of a triangle, and D uses the wrong quantity.
